title: FLASHATTENTION: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
## **FLASHATTENTION: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness**

**Tri Dao**[†] **, Daniel Y. Fu**[†] **, Stefano Ermon**[†] **, Atri Rudra**[‡] **, Christopher Ré**[†]

† Department of Computer Science, Stanford University

‡ Department of Computer Science and Engineering, University at Buffalo, SUNY
`{trid,danfu}@stanford.edu` , `ermon@stanford.edu` , `atri@buffalo.edu` , `chrismre@cs.stanford.edu`

## **Abstract**

Transformers are slow and memory-hungry on long sequences, since the time and memory complexity of self-attention are quadratic in sequence length. Approximate attention methods have attempted to address this problem by trading off model quality to reduce the compute complexity, but often do not achieve wall-clock speedup. We argue that a missing principle is making attention algorithms _IO-aware_ — accounting for reads and writes between levels of GPU memory. We propose FLASHATTENTION, an IO-aware exact attention algorithm that uses tiling to reduce the number of memory reads/writes between GPU high bandwidth memory (HBM) and GPU on-chip SRAM. We analyze the IO complexity of FLASHATTENTION, showing that it requires fewer HBM accesses than standard attention, and is optimal for a range of SRAM sizes. We also extend FLASHATTENTION to block-sparse attention, yielding an approximate attention algorithm that is faster than any existing approximate attention method. FLASHATTENTION trains Transformers faster than existing baselines: 15% end-to-end wall-clock speedup on BERT-large (seq. length 512) compared to the MLPerf 1.1 training speed record, 3× speedup on GPT-2 (seq. length 1K), and 2.4× speedup on long-range arena (seq. length 1K-4K). FLASHATTENTION and block-sparse FLASHATTENTION enable longer context in Transformers, yielding higher quality models (0.7 better perplexity on GPT-2 and 6.4 points of lift on long-document classification) and entirely new capabilities: the first Transformers to achieve better-than-chance performance on the Path-X challenge (seq. length 16K, 61.4% accuracy) and Path-256 (seq. length 64K, 63.1% accuracy).

## **1 Introduction**

Transformer models [86] have emerged as the most widely used architecture in applications such as natural language processing and image classification. Transformers have grown larger [5] and deeper [87], but equipping them with longer context remains difficult [83], since the self-attention module at their heart has time and memory complexity quadratic in sequence length. An important question is whether making attention faster and more memory-efficient can help Transformer models address their runtime and memory challenges for long sequences.

Many approximate attention methods have aimed to reduce the compute and memory requirements of attention. These methods range from sparse-approximation [53, 77] to low-rank approximation [13, 52, 88], and their combinations [3, 9, 96]. Although these methods reduce the compute requirements to linear or near-linear in sequence length, many of them do not display wall-clock speedup against standard attention and have not gained wide adoption. One main reason is that they focus on FLOP reduction (which may not correlate with wall-clock speed) and tend to ignore overheads from memory access (IO).

In this paper, we argue that a missing principle is making attention algorithms _IO-aware_ [1]—that is, carefully accounting for reads and writes to different levels of fast and slow memory (e.g., between fast GPU on-chip SRAM and relatively slow GPU high bandwidth memory, or HBM [47], Figure 1

In the outer loop (red arrows), FLASHATTENTION loops through blocks of the **K** and **V** matrices and loads them to fast on-chip SRAM. left). On modern GPUs, compute speed has out-paced memory speed [64–66], and most operations in Transformers are bottlenecked by memory accesses [45]. IO-aware algorithms have been critical for similar memory-bound operations, when reading and writing data can account for a large portion of the runtime—such as database joins [74], image processing [73], numerical linear algebra [4], and more [42, 89]. However, common Python interfaces to deep learning such as PyTorch and Tensorflow do not allow fine-grained control of memory access.

We propose FLASHATTENTION, a new attention algorithm that computes exact attention with far fewer memory accesses. Our main goal is to avoid reading and writing the attention matrix to and from HBM. This requires (i) computing the softmax reduction without access to the whole input (ii) not storing the large intermediate attention matrix for the backward pass. We apply two well-established techniques to address these challenges. (i) We restructure the attention computation to split the input into blocks and make several passes over input blocks, thus incrementally performing the softmax reduction (also known as **tiling** ). (ii) We store the softmax normalization factor from the forward pass to quickly **recompute** attention on-chip in the backward pass, which is faster than the standard approach of reading the intermediate attention matrix from HBM. We implement FLASHATTENTION in CUDA to achieve fine-grained control over memory access and fuse all the attention operations into one GPU kernel. Even with the increased FLOPs due to recomputation, our algorithm both **runs faster** (up to 7.6x on GPT-2 [70], Figure 1 right) and **uses less memory** —linear in sequence length—than standard attention, thanks to the massively reduced amount of HBM access.

We analyze the IO complexity [1] of FLASHATTENTION, proving that it requires _𝑂_ ( _𝑁_[2] _𝑑_[2] _𝑀_[−][1] ) HBM accesses where _𝑑_ is the head dimension and _𝑀_ is the size of SRAM, as compared to Ω( _𝑁𝑑_ + _𝑁_[2] ) of standard attention. For typical values of _𝑑_ and _𝑀_ , FLASHATTENTION requires many times fewer HBM accesses compared to standard attention (up to 9× fewer, as shown in Fig. 2). Moreover, we provide a lower bound, showing that no exact attention algorithm can asymptotically improve on the number of HBM accesses over all SRAM sizes.

We also show that FLASHATTENTION can serve as a useful primitive for realizing the potential of approximate attention algorithms by overcoming their issues with memory access overhead. As a proof of concept, we implement block-sparse FLASHATTENTION, a sparse attention algorithm that is 2-4× faster than even FLASHATTENTION, scaling up to sequence length of 64k. We prove that block-sparse FLASHATTENTION has better IO complexity than FLASHATTENTION by a factor proportional to the sparsity ratio. We discuss further extensions to other operations (attention on multi-GPU, kernel regression, block-sparse matrix multiply) in Section 5. We open-source FLASHATTENTION to make it easier to build on this primitive[1] .

> 1FLASHATTENTION code is available at `https://github.com/HazyResearch/flash-attention`

2

We empirically validate that FLASHATTENTION speeds up model training and improves model quality by modeling longer context. We also benchmark the runtime and memory footprint of FLASHATTENTION and block-sparse FLASHATTENTION compared to prior attention implementations.

- **Faster Model Training.** FLASHATTENTION trains Transformer models faster in wall-clock time. We train BERT-large (seq. length 512) 15% faster than the training speed record in MLPerf 1.1 [60], GPT2 (seq. length 1K) 3× faster than baseline implementations from HuggingFace [91] and Megatron-LM [80], and long-range arena (seq. length 1K-4K) 2.4× faster than baselines.

- **Higher Quality Models.** FLASHATTENTION scales Transformers to longer sequences, which improves their quality and enables new capabilities. We observe a 0.7 improvement in perplexity on GPT-2 and 6.4 points of lift from modeling longer sequences on long-document classification [14]. FLASHATTENTION enables the first Transformer that can achieve better-than-chance performance on the Path-X [83] challenge, solely from using a longer sequence length (16K). Block-sparse FLASHATTENTION enables a Transformer to scale to even longer sequences (64K), resulting in the first model that can achieve better-than-chance performance on Path-256.

- **Benchmarking Attention.** FLASHATTENTION is up to 3× faster than the standard attention implementation across common sequence lengths from 128 to 2K and scales up to 64K. Up to sequence length of 512, FLASHATTENTION is both faster and more memory-efficient than any existing attention method, whereas for sequence length beyond 1K, some approximate attention methods (e.g., Linformer) start to become faster. On the other hand, block-sparse FLASHATTENTION is faster than all existing approximate attention methods that we know of.

## **2 Background**

We provide some background on the performance characteristics of common deep learning operations on modern hardware (GPUs). We also describe the standard implementation of attention.

## **2.1 Hardware Performance**

We focus here on GPUs. Performance on other hardware accelerators are similar [48, 50].

**GPUMemoryHierarchy.** The GPU memory hierarchy (Fig. 1 left) comprises multiple forms of memory of different sizes and speeds, with smaller memory being faster. As an example, the A100 GPU has 40-80GB of high bandwidth memory (HBM) with bandwidth 1.5-2.0TB/s and 192KB of on-chip SRAM per each of 108 streaming multiprocessors with bandwidth estimated around 19TB/s [46, 47]. The on-chip SRAM is an order of magnitude faster than HBM but many orders of magnitude smaller in size. As compute has gotten faster relative to memory speed [64–66], operations are increasingly bottlenecked by memory (HBM) accesses. Thus exploiting fast SRAM becomes more important.
**Execution Model.** GPUs have a massive number of threads to execute an operation (called a kernel). Each kernel loads inputs from HBM to registers and SRAM, computes, then writes outputs to HBM.

**Performance characteristics.** Depending on the balance of computation and memory accesses, operations can be classified as either compute-bound or memory-bound. This is commonly measured by the _arithmetic intensity_ [89], which is the number of arithmetic operations per byte of memory access.

1. Compute-bound: the time taken by the operation is determined by how many arithmetic operations there are, while time accessing HBM is much smaller. Typical examples are matrix multiply with large inner dimension, and convolution with large number of channels.

2. Memory-bound: the time taken by the operation is determined by the number of memory accesses, while time spent in computation is much smaller. Examples include most other operations: elementwise (e.g., activation, dropout), and reduction (e.g., sum, softmax, batch norm, layer norm).

**Kernel fusion.** The most common approach to accelerate memory-bound operations is kernel fusion: if there are multiple operations applied to the same input, the input can be loaded once from HBM, instead of multiple times for each operation. Compilers can automatically fuse many elementwise operations [55, 68, 78]. However, in the context of model training, the intermediate values still need to be written to HBM to save for the backward pass, reducing the effectiveness of naive kernel fusion.

## **2.2 Standard Attention Implementation**

Given input sequences **Q** _,_ **K** _,_ **V** ∈ R _[𝑁]_[×] _[𝑑]_ where _𝑁_ is the sequence length and _𝑑_ is the head dimension, we want to compute the attention output **O** ∈ R _[𝑁]_[×] _[𝑑]_ : **S** = **QK**[⊤] ∈ R _[𝑁]_[×] _[𝑁] ,_ **P** = softmax( **S** ) ∈ R _[𝑁]_[×] _[𝑁] ,_ **O** = **PV** ∈ R _[𝑁]_[×] _[𝑑] ,_

3

where softmax is applied row-wise.

Standard attention implementations materialize the matrices **S** and **P** to HBM, which takes _𝑂_ ( _𝑁_[2] ) memory. Often _𝑁_ ≫ _𝑑_ (e.g., for GPT2, _𝑁_ = 1024 and _𝑑_ = 64). We describe the standard attention implementation in Algorithm 0. As some or most of the operations are memory-bound (e.g., softmax), the large number of memory accesses translates to slow wall-clock time.

This problem is exacerbated by other elementwise operations applied to the attention matrix, such as masking applied to **S** or dropout applied to **P** . As a result, there have been many attempts to fuse several elementwise operations, such as fusing masking with softmax [80].

In Section 3.2, we will show that the standard attention implementation performs HBM accesses quadratic in the sequence length _𝑁_ . We also compare the number of FLOPs and number of HBM accesses of standard attention and of our method (FLASHATTENTION).

**Algorithm 0** Standard Attention Implementation

**Require:** Matrices **Q** _,_ **K** _,_ **V** ∈ R _[𝑁]_[×] _[𝑑]_ in HBM.

- 1: Load **Q** _,_ **K** by blocks from HBM, compute **S** = **QK**[⊤] , write **S** to HBM.

- 2: Read **S** from HBM, compute **P** = softmax( **S** ), write **P** to HBM.

- 3: Load **P** and **V** by blocks from HBM, compute **O** = **PV** , write **O** to HBM.

- 4: Return **O** .

## **3 FLASHATTENTION: Algorithm, Analysis, and Extensions**

We show how to compute exact attention with fewer HBM reads/writes and without storing large intermediate matrices for the backward pass. This yields an attention algorithm that is both memory efficient and faster in wall-clock time. We analyze its IO complexity, showing that our method requires much fewer HBM accesses compared to standard attention. We further show that FLASHATTENTION can serve as a useful primitive by extending it to handle block-sparse attention.
We focus here on the forward pass for ease of exposition; Appendix B contains details for the backward.

## **3.1 An Efficient Attention Algorithm With Tiling and Recomputation**

Given the inputs **Q** _,_ **K** _,_ **V** ∈ R _[𝑁]_[×] _[𝑑]_ in HBM, we aim to compute the attention output **O** ∈ R _[𝑁]_[×] _[𝑑]_ and write it to HBM. Our goal is to reduce the amount of HBM accesses (to sub-quadratic in _𝑁_ ).

We apply two established techniques (tiling, recomputation) to overcome the technical challenge of computing exact attention in sub-quadratic HBM accesses. We describe this in Algorithm 1. The main idea is that we split the inputs **Q** _,_ **K** _,_ **V** into blocks, load them from slow HBM to fast SRAM, then compute the attention output with respect to those blocks. By scaling the output of each block by the right normalization factor before adding them up, we get the correct result at the end.

**Tiling.** We compute attention by blocks. Softmax couples columns of **K** , so we decompose the large softmax with scaling [53, 62, 69]. For numerical stability, the softmax of vector _𝑥_ ∈ R _[𝐵]_ is computed:

Therefore if we keep track of some extra statistics ( _𝑚_ ( _𝑥_ ) _,ℓ_ ( _𝑥_ )), we can compute softmax one block at a time.[2] We thus split the inputs **Q** _,_ **K** _,_ **V** into blocks (Algorithm 1 line 1), compute the softmax values along with extra statistics (Algorithm 1 line 1), and combine the results (Algorithm 1 line 1).

**Recomputation.** One of our goals is to not store _𝑂_ ( _𝑁_[2] ) intermediate values for the backward pass. The backward pass typically requires the matrices **S** _,_ **P** ∈ R _[𝑁]_[×] _[𝑁]_ to compute the gradients with respect to **Q** _,_ **K** _,_ **V** . However, by storing the output **O** and the softmax normalization statistics ( _𝑚,ℓ_ ), we can recompute the attention matrix **S** and **P** easily in the backward pass from blocks of **Q** _,_ **K** _,_ **V** in SRAM. This can be seen as a form of selective gradient checkpointing [10, 36]. While gradient

In contrast, even with more FLOPs, our recomputation speeds up the backward pass due to reduced HBM accesses (Fig. 2). The full backward pass description is in Appendix B.

**Implementation details: Kernel fusion.** Tiling enables us to implement our algorithm in one CUDA kernel, loading input from HBM, performing all the computation steps (matrix multiply, softmax, optionally masking and dropout, matrix multiply), then write the result back to HBM (masking and dropout in Appendix B). This avoids repeatedly reading and writing of inputs and outputs from and to HBM.

## **3.2 Analysis: IO Complexity of FLASHATTENTION**

We analyze the IO complexity of FLASHATTENTION, showing significant reduction in HBM accesses compared to standard attention. We also provide a lower bound, proving that no exact attention algorithm can asymptotically improve on HBM accesses over all SRAM sizes. Proofs are in Appendix C.

**Theorem 2.** _Let 𝑁 be the sequence length, 𝑑 be the head dimension, and 𝑀 be size of SRAM with 𝑑_ ≤ _𝑀_ ≤ _𝑁𝑑. Standard attention (Algorithm 0) requires_ Θ( _𝑁𝑑_ + _𝑁_[2] ) _HBM accesses, while_ FLASHATTENTION _(Algorithm 1) requires_ Θ( _𝑁_[2] _𝑑_[2] _𝑀_[−][1] ) _HBM accesses._

For typical values of _𝑑_ (64-128) and _𝑀_ (around 100KB), _𝑑_[2] is many times smaller than _𝑀_ , and thus FLASHATTENTION requires many times fewer HBM accesses than standard implementation. This leads to both faster execution and lower memory footprint, which we validate in Section 4.3.

The main idea of the proof is that given the SRAM size of _𝑀_ , we can load blocks of **K** _,_ **V** of size Θ( _𝑀_ ) each (Algorithm 1 line 1). For each block of **K** and **V** , we iterate over all blocks of **Q** (Algorithm 1 line 1) to compute the intermediate values, resulting in Θ( _𝑁𝑑𝑀_[−][1] ) passes over **Q** . Each pass loads Θ( _𝑁𝑑_ ) elements, which amounts to Θ( _𝑁_[2] _𝑑_[2] _𝑀_[−][1] ) HBM accesses. We similarly prove that the backward pass of standard attention requires Θ( _𝑁𝑑_ + _𝑁_[2] ) HBM accesses while the backward pass of FLASHATTENTION requires Θ( _𝑁_[2] _𝑑_[2] _𝑀_[−][1] ) HBM accesses (Appendix B).
We prove a lower-bound: one cannot asymptotically improve on the number of HBM accesses for all values of _𝑀_ (the SRAM size) when computing exact attention.

**Proposition 3.** _Let 𝑁 be the sequence length, 𝑑 be the head dimension, and 𝑀 be size of SRAM with 𝑑_ ≤ _𝑀_ ≤ _𝑁𝑑. There does not exist an algorithm to compute exact attention with 𝑜_ ( _𝑁_[2] _𝑑_[2] _𝑀_[−][1] ) _HBM accesses for all 𝑀 in the range_ [ _𝑑,𝑁𝑑_ ] _._

The proof relies on the fact that for _𝑀_ =Θ( _𝑁𝑑_ ) any algorithm must perform Ω( _𝑁_[2] _𝑑_[2] _𝑀_[−][1] ) =Ω( _𝑁𝑑_ ) HBM accesses. This type of lower bound over a subrange of _𝑀_ is common in the streaming algorithms literature [92]. We leave proving parameterized complexity [29] lower bounds in terms of _𝑀_ as exciting future work.

We validate that the number of HBM accesses is the main determining factor of attention run-time. In Fig. 2 (left), we see that even though FLASHATTENTION has higher FLOP count compared to standard attention (due to recomputation in the backward pass), it has much fewer HBM accesses, resulting in much faster runtime. In Fig. 2 (middle), we vary the block size _𝐵𝑐_ of FLASHATTENTION, which results in different amounts of HBM accesses, and measure the runtime of the forward pass. As block size increases, the number of HBM accesses decreases (as we make fewer passes over the input), and runtime decreases. For large enough block size (beyond 256), the runtime is then bottlenecked by other factors (e.g., arithmetic operations). Moreover, larger block size will not fit into the small SRAM size.

## **3.3 Extension: Block-Sparse FLASHATTENTION**

We extend FLASHATTENTION to approximate attention: we propose block-sparse FLASHATTENTION, whose IO complexity is smaller than FLASHATTENTION by a factor proportional to the sparsity. Given inputs **Q** _,_ **K** _,_ **V** ∈ R _[𝑁]_[×] _[𝑑]_ and a mask matrix **M**[˜] ∈{0 _,_ 1} _[𝑁]_[×] _[𝑁]_ , we want to compute:

Given a predefined block sparsity mask **M** ∈{0 _,_ 1} _[𝑁]_[/] _[𝐵][𝑟]_[×] _[𝑁]_[/] _[𝐵][𝑐]_ we can easily adapt Algorithm 1 to only compute the nonzero blocks of the attention matrix. The algorithm is identical to Algorithm 1, except we skip zero blocks. We reproduce the algorithm description in Algorithm 5 in Appendix B.

We also analyze the IO complexity of block-sparse FLASHATTENTION.

**Proposition 4.** _Let 𝑁 be the sequence length, 𝑑 be the head dimension, and 𝑀 be size of SRAM with 𝑑_ ≤ _𝑀_ ≤ _𝑁𝑑. Block-sparse_ FLASHATTENTION _(Algorithm 5) requires_ Θ( _𝑁𝑑_ + _𝑁_[2] _𝑑_[2] _𝑀_[−][1] _𝑠_ ) _HBM accesses where 𝑠 is the fraction of nonzero blocks in the block-sparsity mask._

We see that applying block-sparsity yields a direct improvement by the sparsity to the larger term in the IO complexity. ## **4 Experiments**

We evaluate the impact of using FLASHATTENTION to train Transformer models. We validate two claims about training time and model accuracy, and report attention runtime and memory benchmarks.

6

- **Training Speed.** FLASHATTENTION outperforms the MLPerf 1.1 [60] speed record for BERT by 15%, and speeds up GPT-2 up to 3× over HuggingFace [91] and 1 _._ 8× over Megatron [80] over standard Transformers. FLASHATTENTION speeds up the long-range arena (LRA) benchmark 2.4×.

- **Quality.** FLASHATTENTION scales Transformers to longer sequences, yielding higher quality. FLASHATTENTION trains GPT-2 with context length 4K faster than Megatron trains GPT-2 with context length 1K, while achieving 0.7 better perplexity. Modeling longer sequences yields 6.4 points of lift on two long-document classification tasks. Finally, FLASHATTENTION yields the **first Transformer** that can achieve better-than-random performance on the challenging Path-X task (sequence length 16K), and block-sparse FLASHATTENTION yields the **first sequence model** that we know of that can achieve better-than-random performance on Path-256 (sequence length 64K).

- **Benchmarking Attention.** We measure the runtime and memory performance of FLASHATTENTION and block-sparse FLASHATTENTION based on sequence length. We confirm that the memory footprint of FLASHATTENTION scales linearly with seq. length and is up to 3× faster than standard attention for common seq. lengths (up to 2K). We confirm that runtime of block-sparse FLASHATTENTION scales linearly in seq. length and is faster than all existing approximate attention baselines.

Additional experiment details are in Appendix E.

**Path-X and Path-256.** The Path-X and Path-256 benchmarks are challenging tasks from the long-range arena benchmark designed to test long context. The task is to classify whether two points in a black and white 128×128 (or 256×256) image have a path connecting them, and the images are fed to the transformer one pixel at a time. ## **4.3 Benchmarking Attention**

We vary sequence length and measure runtime and memory usage of FLASHATTENTION and block-sparse FLASHATTENTION against various attention baselines on one A100 GPU with 40 GB HBM, with dropout and a padding mask. We compare against reference implementations for exact attention, approximate attention, and sparse attention. We report a subset of baselines in the main body; Appendix E contains more baselines and full details.

**Runtime.** Figure 3 (left) reports the runtime in milliseconds of the forward + backward pass of FLASHATTENTION and block-sparse FLASHATTENTION compared to the baselines in exact, approximate, and sparse attention (exact numbers in Appendix E). Runtime grows quadratically with sequence length, but FLASHATTENTION runs significantly faster than **exact attention** baselines, up to 3× faster than the PyTorch implementation. The runtimes of many approximate/sparse attention mechanisms grow linearly with sequence length, but FLASHATTENTION still runs faster than approximate and sparse attention for short sequences due to fewer memory accesses. The **approximate attention** runtimes begin to cross over with FLASHATTENTION at sequences between 512 and 1024. On the other hand, block-sparse FLASHATTENTION is faster than all implementations of exact, sparse, and approximate attention that we know of, across all sequence lengths.

**Memory Footprint.** Figure 3 (right) shows the memory footprint of FLASHATTENTION and block-sparse FLASHATTENTION compared to various exact, approximate, and sparse attention baselines. FLASHATTENTION and block-sparse FLASHATTENTION have the same memory footprint, which grows linearly with sequence length. FLASHATTENTION is up to 20× more memory efficient than **exact attention** baselines, and is more memory-efficient than the **approximate attention** baselines. All other algorithms except for Linformer run out of memory on an A100 GPU before 64K, and FLASHATTENTION is still 2× more efficient than Linformer.

## **5 Limitations and Future Directions**

We discuss limitations of our approach and future directions. Related work is given in Appendix A.

**Compiling to CUDA.** Our current approach to building IO-aware implementations of attention requires writing a new CUDA kernel for each new attention implementation. This requires writing the attention algorithm in a considerably lower-level language than PyTorch, and requires significant engineering effort. Implementations may also not be transferrable across GPU architectures. These limitations suggest the need for a method that supports writing attention algorithms in a high-level language (e.g., PyTorch), and compiling to IO-aware implementations in CUDA—similar to efforts such as Halide in image processing [73].

**IO-Aware Deep Learning.** We believe that the IO-aware approach can extend beyond attention. Attention is the most memory-intensive computation in Transformers, but every layer in a deep network touches GPU HBM. We hope our work inspires IO-aware implementations of additional modules. We discuss these potential extensions in Appendix D.
**Multi-GPU IO-Aware Methods.** Our IO-aware implementation of attention is optimal within constants for computing attention on a single GPU. However, the attention computation may be par-
> 4Path-256 requires longer sequences but has relatively shorter paths than Path-X, so it is easier to obtain a higher accuracy.

9

allelizable across multiple GPUs [75]. Using multiple GPUs adds an additional layer to IO analysis— accounting for data transfer between GPUs. We hope our work inspires future work in this direction.

**Societal Impacts.** As Transformer-based foundation models grow in size and data, our work seeks to understand how to train these large models more efficiently. This may allow a general community with limited access to computational resources to train and understand those foundation models. Our method is applicable to all Transformer-based models, which have a variety of applications, both positive and negative. For example, language modeling may make it easier to spread misinformation, while image classification models may make automatic surveillance easier. Alleviating these risks requires addressing application-specific issues such as privacy, bias, and discrimination.

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: This paper presents an exceptionally high-quality contribution to the field of deep learning systems, offering a compelling paradigm shift from FLOP-reduction to IO-awareness in attention mechanisms. The clarity of the writing is excellent; the authors identify a precise technical bottleneck—the memory wall between GPU HBM and SRAM—and provide a principled architectural solution via tiling and recomputation. The most significant strength of the paper lies in its "exactness"—achieving massive performance gains without the fidelity trade-offs inherent in prior approximation methods—and its impressive empirical validation across diverse tasks like Path-X. If there is a minor concern, it is the increased complexity of the implementation, as the "fine-grained control" mentioned requires custom CUDA kernels that are harder to maintain than standard high-level framework code. However, the theoretical IO complexity analysis and the provided lower bounds strongly substantiate the algorithm’s optimality. Overall, this is a seminal work that effectively bridges the gap between hardware constraints and algorithmic design.

comments.explanation: Looking at the IO complexity analysis in Section 3.2 and Theorem 2, there is a potential discrepancy in the power of the head dimension $d$. The text states $O(N^2 d^2 M^{-1})$, but standard IO-aware matrix multiplication (which $QK^T$ and $PV$ resemble) usually scales linearly with the dimension when tiling. In Algorithm 1, the block size $B_c$ and $B_r$ are set to $\approx M/d$. When we multiply a block of $Q$ ($B_r \times d$) by a block of $K$ ($B_c \times d$), and we do this $N/B_r \times N/B_c$ times, the number of HBM accesses typically evaluates to $O(N^2 d / M + Nd)$. If $d$ is squared ($d^2$), it suggests a different memory layout or a less efficient tiling than what is usually achievable. However, the authors later mention 'for typical values of $d$ (64-128)... $d^2$ is many times smaller than $M$', which suggests the $d^2$ term is intentional in their derivation to account for the total sub-matrix size or specific tiling constraints. While it might be a subtle OCR misread of $O(N^2 d / M)$, the consistency between the prose and the theorem suggests it is the authors' intended derivation for this specific attention fusion.

comments.explanation: The abstract and introduction claim that FlashAttention is 'exact attention' and 'uses less memory—linear in sequence length—than standard attention.' This is a significant claim. However, the benchmarking summary notes that for sequences beyond 1K, approximate methods like Linformer 'start to become faster.' There is a slight ambiguity here: elsewhere the paper claims block-sparse FlashAttention (their approximate version) is 'faster than any existing approximate attention method.' This creates a tension where the 'exact' version is compared to 'approximate' methods in one sentence, potentially underselling the block-sparse variant's performance. A reader might be confused as to whether FlashAttention is being positioned as a replacement for approximate methods in terms of speed, or if that role is reserved solely for the block-sparse version.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Computational Speedup' vs 'FLOPs Increase'
comments.quote: Even with the increased FLOPs due to recomputation, our algorithm both **runs faster** (up to 7.6x on GPT-2 [70], Figure 1 right) and **uses less memory**
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify that recomputation (gradient checkpointing) increases the total operation count (FLOPs). However, in Section 3.1 (Algorithm 1) and Theorem 1, they state the complexity is $O(N^2 d)$ FLOPs—the same as standard attention. While $O$-notation hides constants, the recomputation in the backward pass effectively doubles the arithmetic work for the attention matrix calculation. For a reader trying to reconcile the 'increased FLOPs' mentioned in the prose with the 'standard $O(N^2 d)$' in the theorem, more explicit mention of the constant factor (e.g., that it is $2 \times$ or $3 \times$ more work but $10 \times$ fewer IOs) would be helpful to substantiate why a 'compute-heavy' approach is faster on memory-bound hardware.

comments.title: Self-contradictory logic in Theorem 2 derivation
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the text explains the logic behind Theorem 2 by stating: 'For each block of K and V, we iterate over all blocks of Q... resulting in Θ(NdM⁻¹) passes over Q.' However, earlier in Section 3.1 and within Algorithm 1 itself (line 5), the algorithm is structured with K and V in the outer loop (Tc blocks) and Q in the inner loop (Tr blocks). Tc is defined as N/Bc, where Bc ≈ M/d. Therefore, the number of passes over Q is indeed N / (M/d) = Nd/M. Each pass loads all Tr blocks of Q, total size Nd. Multiplying the number of passes (Nd/M) by the size of Q (Nd) yields (Nd/M) * Nd = N²d²/M. This matches the complexity in Theorem 2. However, the manual calculation provided in the paragraph text claims: 'Each pass loads Θ(Nd) elements, which amounts to Θ(N²d²M⁻¹) HBM accesses.' In standard complexity notation, if you have Nd/M passes and each pass is Nd, the total is N²d²/M. Comparing this to the 'Standard Attention' complexity of N² (dropping d for a moment), the text later claims this is 'up to 9x fewer' HBM accesses. If d=64 and M is large (e.g., 100KB), N²d²/M could actually be *larger* than N² if the constants or the d² factor are not handled carefully. Specifically, 64² is 4096. If M is 100KB (approx 25k float32 elements), then d²/M is 4096/25000 ≈ 0.16. This provides a reduction, but the prose explanation of 'many times fewer' relies on d² being much smaller than M, which is a tight margin for typical GPU caches. More importantly, the derivation steps in the prose are logically sound but the result is sensitive to d², which is often ignored in high-level transformer complexity discussions.

comments.title: Incorrect block size calculation for Br
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1 line 1, the authors define Kr and Kc (block sizes). Specifically, Br is defined as 'min([float] 4M/d, d)'. In the context of the attention mechanism, Br refers to the number of rows of Q loaded into SRAM. If d is the head dimension (e.g., 64), setting the block size Br to the minimum of 4M/d and d seems like a typo or a severe under-utilization of SRAM. If M is ~100KB and d=64, 4M/d is a large number (~6400), while d is only 64. Forcing the block size to be as small as 'd' (64) when the SRAM could hold thousands of rows of Q is inefficient and contradicts the goal of maximizing tiling to reduce HBM passes. It is more likely the authors intended the minimum to be with N (the sequence length) or simply to use the full capacity of SRAM for Br to minimize the outer loop iterations. Restricting Br to d suggests a square block constraint that isn't justified by the memory hierarchy requirements.

comments.quote: Block-sparse FLASHATTENTION (Algorithm 5) requires $\Theta(Nd + N^{2} d^{2} M^{-1} s)$ HBM accesses where $s$ is the fraction of nonzero blocks in the block-sparsity mask.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.3, the text defines 's' as the fraction of nonzero blocks (e.g., $s=0.1$ for 10% density). However, the stated IO complexity $\Theta(Nd + N^{2} d^{2} M^{-1} s)$ implies that as $s$ (the fraction of nonzero blocks) decreases to zero, the HBM accesses decrease. This is logically sound if $s$ is density. However, the prose in the very next paragraph and the caption of Figure 2 use 'sparsity' and 's' interchangeably in a confusing way. Figure 2 (right) says speedup is proportional to 'sparsity', but usually, higher sparsity means a lower fraction of non-zeros. If $s$ is the *sparsity ratio* (e.g., 90%), the formula would be $\Theta(...(1-s))$. The paper likely means $s$ is the 'density' or 'occupancy', but calling it 'sparsity' in the prose and then using it as a direct multiplier in the complexity $O(N^2 s)$ is a common but confusing notation inconsistency in the literature.

comments.title: Ambiguity in block size constraint and SRAM capacity
comments.quote: Set block sizes $B_c = \lceil \frac{4M}{d} \rceil, B_r = \min(\lceil \frac{4M}{d} \rceil, d)$.
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, the block sizes $B_c$ and $B_r$ are defined relative to $M$ (SRAM size) and $d$ (head dimension). If $M$ is the total SRAM capacity in bytes (or words), the formula $4M/d$ seems to imply a dimensional mismatch or a missing constant factor regarding the size of the data types (e.g., FP16 or FP32). More importantly, the constraints in the text state $d \le M$, and the algorithm loads blocks of $\mathbf{K, V}$ of size $B_c \times d$. If $B_c \approx 4M/d$, then the size of one block is $(4M/d) \times d = 4M$. Loading a $4M$ sized block into an $M$ sized SRAM is physically impossible. Looking at the logic of tiling, the sum of the sizes of the blocks loaded ($\mathbf{Q}_i, \mathbf{K}_j, \mathbf{V}_j$ plus statistics) must be $\le M$. The formula provided in the OCR text ($\frac{4M}{d}$) is likely an OCR error for something like $\frac{M}{4d}$, or the authors have defined $M$ in a non-standard way. As written, the algorithm would exceed SRAM capacity by a factor of ~16.

comments.title: Potential overstatement of speedup vs. approximate methods
comments.quote: FLASHTATTENTION is both faster and more memory-efficient than any existing attention method, whereas for sequence length beyond 1K, some approximate attention methods (e.g., Linformer) start to become faster.
comments.explanation: The abstract and introduction claim FLASHATTENTION is 'faster than any existing approximate attention method' (specifically referring to the block-sparse version). However, the benchmarking summary in Section 4 admits that at $N > 1024$, standard approximate methods like Linformer 'start to become faster'. While the authors later qualify this by saying *block-sparse* FLASHATTENTION is faster than those, the blanket statement in the abstract—which claims FLASHATTENTION (not strictly the block-sparse version) is faster than all existing approximate methods—is slightly contradicted by the specific benchmarking results for sequence lengths where $O(N)$ methods like Linformer or Performer theoretically cross the $O(N^2)$ FLASHATTENTION curve.

progressive_original__gemini-3-flash-pre.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3-flash-pre.overall_feedback: This paper presents an exceptionally high-quality contribution to the field of deep learning systems, offering a compelling paradigm shift from FLOP-reduction to IO-awareness in attention mechanisms. The clarity of the writing is excellent; the authors identify a precise technical bottleneck—the memory wall between GPU HBM and SRAM—and provide a principled architectural solution via tiling and recomputation. The most significant strength of the paper lies in its "exactness"—achieving massive performance gains without the fidelity trade-offs inherent in prior approximation methods—and its impressive empirical validation across diverse tasks like Path-X. If there is a minor concern, it is the increased complexity of the implementation, as the "fine-grained control" mentioned requires custom CUDA kernels that are harder to maintain than standard high-level framework code. However, the theoretical IO complexity analysis and the provided lower bounds strongly substantiate the algorithm’s optimality. Overall, this is a seminal work that effectively bridges the gap between hardware constraints and algorithmic design.

comments.title: Possible OCR Error in IO Complexity Big-O Bound
comments.quote: proving that it requires _𝑂_ ( _𝑁_[2] _𝑑_[2] _𝑀_[−][1] ) HBM accesses where _𝑑_ is the head dimension and _𝑀_ is the size of SRAM, as compared to Ω( _𝑁𝑑_ + _𝑁_[2] ) of standard attention.
comments.explanation: Looking at the IO complexity analysis in Section 3.2 and Theorem 2, there is a potential discrepancy in the power of the head dimension $d$. The text states $O(N^2 d^2 M^{-1})$, but standard IO-aware matrix multiplication (which $QK^T$ and $PV$ resemble) usually scales linearly with the dimension when tiling. In Algorithm 1, the block size $B_c$ and $B_r$ are set to $\approx M/d$. When we multiply a block of $Q$ ($B_r \times d$) by a block of $K$ ($B_c \times d$), and we do this $N/B_r \times N/B_c$ times, the number of HBM accesses typically evaluates to $O(N^2 d / M + Nd)$. If $d$ is squared ($d^2$), it suggests a different memory layout or a less efficient tiling than what is usually achievable. However, the authors later mention 'for typical values of $d$ (64-128)... $d^2$ is many times smaller than $M$', which suggests the $d^2$ term is intentional in their derivation to account for the total sub-matrix size or specific tiling constraints. While it might be a subtle OCR misread of $O(N^2 d / M)$, the consistency between the prose and the theorem suggests it is the authors' intended derivation for this specific attention fusion.

comments.title: Potential Contradiction regarding Memory Usage Comparison
comments.quote: Up to sequence length of 512, FLASHATTENTION is both faster and more memory-efficient than any existing attention method, whereas for sequence length beyond 1K, some approximate attention methods (e.g., Linformer) start to become faster.
comments.explanation: The abstract and introduction claim that FlashAttention is 'exact attention' and 'uses less memory—linear in sequence length—than standard attention.' This is a significant claim. However, the benchmarking summary notes that for sequences beyond 1K, approximate methods like Linformer 'start to become faster.' There is a slight ambiguity here: elsewhere the paper claims block-sparse FlashAttention (their approximate version) is 'faster than any existing approximate attention method.' This creates a tension where the 'exact' version is compared to 'approximate' methods in one sentence, potentially underselling the block-sparse variant's performance. A reader might be confused as to whether FlashAttention is being positioned as a replacement for approximate methods in terms of speed, or if that role is reserved solely for the block-sparse version.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Computational Speedup' vs 'FLOPs Increase'
comments.quote: Even with the increased FLOPs due to recomputation, our algorithm both **runs faster** (up to 7.6x on GPT-2 [70], Figure 1 right) and **uses less memory**
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify that recomputation (gradient checkpointing) increases the total operation count (FLOPs). However, in Section 3.1 (Algorithm 1) and Theorem 1, they state the complexity is $O(N^2 d)$ FLOPs—the same as standard attention. While $O$-notation hides constants, the recomputation in the backward pass effectively doubles the arithmetic work for the attention matrix calculation. For a reader trying to reconcile the 'increased FLOPs' mentioned in the prose with the 'standard $O(N^2 d)$' in the theorem, more explicit mention of the constant factor (e.g., that it is $2 \times$ or $3 \times$ more work but $10 \times$ fewer IOs) would be helpful to substantiate why a 'compute-heavy' approach is faster on memory-bound hardware.

comments.title: Self-contradictory logic in Theorem 2 derivation
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the text explains the logic behind Theorem 2 by stating: 'For each block of K and V, we iterate over all blocks of Q... resulting in Θ(NdM⁻¹) passes over Q.' However, earlier in Section 3.1 and within Algorithm 1 itself (line 5), the algorithm is structured with K and V in the outer loop (Tc blocks) and Q in the inner loop (Tr blocks). Tc is defined as N/Bc, where Bc ≈ M/d. Therefore, the number of passes over Q is indeed N / (M/d) = Nd/M. Each pass loads all Tr blocks of Q, total size Nd. Multiplying the number of passes (Nd/M) by the size of Q (Nd) yields (Nd/M) * Nd = N²d²/M. This matches the complexity in Theorem 2. However, the manual calculation provided in the paragraph text claims: 'Each pass loads Θ(Nd) elements, which amounts to Θ(N²d²M⁻¹) HBM accesses.' In standard complexity notation, if you have Nd/M passes and each pass is Nd, the total is N²d²/M. Comparing this to the 'Standard Attention' complexity of N² (dropping d for a moment), the text later claims this is 'up to 9x fewer' HBM accesses. If d=64 and M is large (e.g., 100KB), N²d²/M could actually be *larger* than N² if the constants or the d² factor are not handled carefully. Specifically, 64² is 4096. If M is 100KB (approx 25k float32 elements), then d²/M is 4096/25000 ≈ 0.16. This provides a reduction, but the prose explanation of 'many times fewer' relies on d² being much smaller than M, which is a tight margin for typical GPU caches. More importantly, the derivation steps in the prose are logically sound but the result is sensitive to d², which is often ignored in high-level transformer complexity discussions.

comments.title: Incorrect block size calculation for Br
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1 line 1, the authors define Kr and Kc (block sizes). Specifically, Br is defined as 'min([float] 4M/d, d)'. In the context of the attention mechanism, Br refers to the number of rows of Q loaded into SRAM. If d is the head dimension (e.g., 64), setting the block size Br to the minimum of 4M/d and d seems like a typo or a severe under-utilization of SRAM. If M is ~100KB and d=64, 4M/d is a large number (~6400), while d is only 64. Forcing the block size to be as small as 'd' (64) when the SRAM could hold thousands of rows of Q is inefficient and contradicts the goal of maximizing tiling to reduce HBM passes. It is more likely the authors intended the minimum to be with N (the sequence length) or simply to use the full capacity of SRAM for Br to minimize the outer loop iterations. Restricting Br to d suggests a square block constraint that isn't justified by the memory hierarchy requirements.

comments.title: Mismatched line references for Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: In Section 3.1, the text states: 'We thus split the inputs Q, K, V into blocks (Algorithm 1 line 1), compute the softmax values along with extra statistics (Algorithm 1 line 1), and combine the results (Algorithm 1 line 1).' All three distinct stages of the algorithm are referenced to 'line 1'. Looking at Algorithm 1, splitting occurs in lines 1-4, computation of local statistics in line 10, and combining/updating occurs in lines 11-12. This is likely an OCR or LaTeX compilation error where the ref labels failed to resolve to the correct line numbers, making the textual walk-through of the algorithm confusing for a reader attempting to map the prose to the pseudo-code.

comments.title: Potential OCR error or notation conflict in Softmax Decomp
comments.explanation: In the passage describing the decomposition of softmax for vectors x(1) and x(2), the text includes the formula: 'softmax(x) = [f]([x]) / l(x) [.]'. In standard notation and earlier in the same paragraph, the softmax was defined as f(x)/l(x). However, the subsequent Algorithm 1 (Line 12) uses a much more complex update rule involving diagonal matrices and exponentials: 'O_i <- diag(l_i_new)^-1 (diag(l_i) e^(m_i - m_i_new) O_i + ...)'. While the update rule in the algorithm is a correct application of the 'Online Softmax' / 'Algebraic Aggregation' technique (keeping the running sum of exponentials), the introductory prose 'softmax(x) = [f]([x]) / l(x)' is a simplified representation that ignores the rescaling required when concatenating blocks with different max values (m). A reader might find the jump from the simple concatenation formula to the matrix algebra in Line 12 difficult to verify without more intermediate steps.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and impactful contribution, addressing the memory and speed bottlenecks of Transformer attention through an elegant IO-aware tiling strategy. The quality is high, with rigorous IO complexity analysis, careful implementation, and strong empirical results demonstrating substantial wall-clock speedups and reduced memory usage. The clarity is generally excellent: the motivation, algorithmic innovation (tiling with online softmax and recomputation), and the distinction from FLOP-oriented approaches are well articulated. However, several issues merit deeper scrutiny. First, the claim of "exact attention" deserves nuance: the numerical stability of the recomputation-based backward pass and the blockwise softmax (which relies on rescaling) should be explicitly addressed, as finite-precision arithmetic could introduce minor deviations from exact attention, especially for long sequences. Second, the IO complexity lower bound relies on assumptions about SRAM size relative to problem dimensions (e.g., \(M = \Omega(d)\)); it is not clear that the bound holds for all practical regimes, and the optimality claim might be overstated. Third, the paper’s focus on NVIDIA GPU HBM/SRAM hierarchy limits generalizability—the approach may not transfer directly to other accelerators (e.g., TPUs) with different memory hierarchies or programming models. Fourth, while the block-sparse extension is a valuable addition, the title’s emphasis on "exact attention" is somewhat misleading since the sparse variant is approximate; a clearer distinction in the abstract or introduction would help. Finally, the end-to-end speedup benchmarks, while impressive, are against specific baselines (e.g., MLPerf 1.1 record, standard PyTorch attention); comparisons with other optimized implementations (e.g., xformers, FasterTransformer) would strengthen the evaluation. Despite these concerns, the paper is a landmark contribution that reorients attention optimization toward memory-access efficiency, and the identified issues are mostly avenues for future refinement rather than fundamental flaws.

comments.title: Incorrect use of Ω for standard attention complexity
comments.quote: as compared to Ω( _𝑁𝑑_ + _𝑁_[2] ) of standard attention.
comments.explanation: The authors state the HBM access complexity of standard attention as Ω(𝑁𝑑 + 𝑁²), using big-Omega which denotes a lower bound. However, the intended meaning is to give an upper bound (or exact bound) for comparison with FlashAttention's upper bound O(𝑁²𝑑²/𝑀). Later in the paper (Theorem 2) the standard attention complexity is correctly given as Θ(𝑁𝑑 + 𝑁²). Using Ω here is technically a lower bound, not an upper bound, which could confuse readers about the direction of the comparison. The context resolves the intended comparison, but the notation is mildly inconsistent with standard usage in complexity analysis.

comments.title: Line break / footnote marker confusion
comments.quote:
> 1FLASHATTENTION code is available at `https://github.com/HazyResearch/flash-attention`

2

We empirically validate that
comments.explanation: The passage begins with '> 1FLASHATTENTION code is available at...' followed by a line with '2' alone, then the prose. This appears to be an artifact where a footnote marker or superscript '1' was separated from its referent text by line breaks or a page break. In the earlier abstract, a similar footnote marker appears as '[1]' in the text. The stray '2' on its own line likely corresponds to a superscript or footnote number that should attach to the preceding code URL or the following sentence. While not a mathematical error, this OCR artifact could confuse a reader about whether '2' is part of the bullet list or a footnote reference. The context makes it clear that the bullet list starting with 'Faster Model Training.' follows, so the '2' is almost certainly a misplaced footnote marker. This is a formatting artifact, not a content error, and does not affect the scientific claims.

comments.title: Incorrect formula for softmax decomposition
comments.explanation: The softmax formula is written as `softmax( x ) := _ℓ[𝑓]_ ([(] _𝑥[𝑥]_ )[)] _[.]_`. The intended meaning is softmax(x) = f(x) / ℓ(x), as standard. The OCR has mangled the expression: the denominator appears to be ℓ(f(x)) rather than ℓ(x). However, from the context and the later consistent usage (e.g., 'softmax( x ) = _[𝑓]_[(] _[𝑥]_[)] _ℓ_ ( _𝑥_ ) _[.]_' for the concatenated case), it is clear the author intends the standard ratio. The issue is purely OCR corruption of the notation; the correct mathematical intent is unambiguous from the surrounding text. No real problem for a careful reader.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of ℓ(x) in the softmax decomposition
comments.explanation: The equation for ℓ(x) when concatenating two vectors introduces an exponentiation in the update, but the initial definition of ℓ(x) was 'ℓ( x ) := ∑︁ _𝑖 f( x )_ i', i.e., the sum of the exponentials (the numerator before normalization). The notation here switches: it writes ℓ(x) for the combined result as a function of ℓ of the parts, which is consistent with the online softmax trick. However, the exponentiation factors in the update are correct (they rescale by the difference in max). The only potential confusion is that the same symbol ℓ is used for both the raw sum-of-exponentials and the updated version; this is standard in the literature. No error.

comments.title: OCR-induced corruption in block size formulas
comments.quote: Set block sizes _𝐵𝑐_ =  4 _𝑀𝑑_  _,𝐵𝑟_ = min[] 4 _[𝑀] 𝑑_  _,𝑑_[] .
comments.explanation: The block size formulas appear garbled. Standard FlashAttention sets B_c = M/4d (or ceiling) and B_r = min(M/4d, d). The symbols and brackets have been mangled by OCR. However, the intended meaning is recoverable from context and the algorithm's subsequent usage (where the inner dimension is d, and blocks need to fit key/value in SRAM). This is an OCR artifact, not an author error. Readers familiar with the paper will recognize the intended formula.

comments.title: Missing floor/ceil notation on block count and inconsistency in division
comments.quote: Divide **Q** into _𝑇𝑟_ =  _𝐵𝑁𝑟_  blocks
comments.explanation: The symbol '' likely represents a floor or ceiling function (e.g., ⌈N/B_r⌉). The OCR has lost the precise symbol. However, the intent is clear: the total number of blocks is the ceiling of the division. Since this is an implementation detail (a ceiling division), it does not affect the asymptotic analysis or correctness proof. Trivial OCR artifact.

comments.title: Potential under-specification of rowmax and rowsum operations
comments.quote: On˜ chip, compute _𝑚𝑖𝑗_ = rowmax( **S** _𝑖𝑗_ ) ∈ R _[𝐵][𝑟]_ , **P**[˜] _𝑖𝑗_ = exp( **S** _𝑖𝑗_ − _𝑚𝑖𝑗_ ) ∈ R _[𝐵][𝑟]_[×] _[𝐵][𝑐]_ (pointwise), _ℓ𝑖𝑗_ = rowsum( ˜ **P** _𝑖𝑗_ ) ∈ R _[𝐵] 𝑟_ .
comments.explanation: The description states 'rowmax( **S** _𝑖𝑗_ )' and 'rowsum( **P** _𝑖𝑗_ )'. A careful reader might wonder: does rowmax return a column vector of maxima per row (correct for softmax scaling)? Or does it return the maximum over the entire block? The context and subsequent use (subtracting _𝑚𝑖𝑗_ from each element of its row) clarify that it is a per-row maximum. The notation '∈ R^{B_r}' confirms it returns a vector of length B_r. This is standard in the softmax tiling literature. No ambiguity for a reader familiar with the technique.

comments.title: Underspecified choice of block sizes relative to SRAM capacity
comments.quote: Set block sizes _𝐵𝑐_ =  4 _𝑀𝑑_  _,𝐵𝑟_ = min[] 4 _[𝑀] 𝑑_  _,𝑑_[] .
comments.explanation: The passage does not explain why the factor of 4 appears in the denominator, nor how much SRAM is allocated for each of Q, K, V, and the intermediate results. A reader trying to implement the algorithm would need to know that the SRAM must simultaneously hold one block of K (B_c × d), one block of V (B_c × d), one block of Q (B_r × d), and the outputs (B_r × d) plus statistics. The factor 4 is a heuristic to ensure everything fits. This is a minor underspecification; it is resolved in the original paper's appendix, which is referenced. Not a serious issue for the passage's purpose.

comments.title: Claim that no exact attention algorithm can improve on HBM accesses over all SRAM sizes may be overstated
comments.quote: Proposition 3. ... There does not exist an algorithm to compute exact attention with 𝑜(𝑁^2 𝑑^2 𝑀^{−1}) HBM accesses for all 𝑀 in the range [𝑑,𝑁𝑑].
comments.explanation: The proposition claims a lower bound over 'all M in the range [d, Nd]'. The proof sketch only considers the case M = Θ(Nd), showing that Ω(Nd) accesses are needed. However, the lower bound form 'o(N^2 d^2 / M)' for that specific M yields Ω(N^2 d^2 / (Nd)) = Ω(Nd), which matches the trivial lower bound. The proposition states 'for all M in the range', but the proof only addresses one endpoint. A true parameterized lower bound would need to show that for any M, the complexity cannot be o(N^2 d^2 / M). The authors acknowledge this limitation ('we leave proving parameterized complexity lower bounds...as exciting future work'), so the proposition as stated is technically correct (it only rules out algorithms that are o(N^2 d^2 / M) *for all M simultaneously*), but the phrasing could mislead a reader into thinking they have proven a tight lower bound for each M individually. This is a minor issue of precision.

comments.title: Unclear usage of block size notation in sparsity mask definition
comments.quote: We require **M**[˜] to have block form: for some block sizes _𝐵𝑟 ,𝐵𝑐_ , for all _𝑘,𝑙_ , **M**[˜] _𝑘,𝑙_ = **M** _𝑖𝑗_ with _𝑖_ = ⌊ _𝑘_ / _𝐵𝑟_ ⌋ _,𝑗_ = ⌊ _𝑙_ / _𝐵𝑐_ ⌋ for some **M** ∈{0 _,_ 1} _[𝑁]_[/] _[𝐵][𝑟]_[×] _[𝑁]_[/] _[𝐵][𝑐]_ .
comments.explanation: The notation is ambiguous about which block sizes are used. Earlier in the paper, _B_c and _B_r are defined as block sizes for tiling in Algorithm 1 (line 1). However, in the context of block-sparse attention, the paper says 'for some block sizes _B_r, _B_c'. It is unclear if these block sizes are the same as the tiling block sizes from Algorithm 1, or if they are independent parameters. If they are the same, the sparsity mask dimensionality _N/B_r × N/B_c_ would be determined by the tiling scheme, which is fine, but the phrase 'for some block sizes' suggests they could be arbitrary. This could confuse a reader trying to implement the method, as they would need to know whether the sparsity pattern's block size must match the tiling block size.

comments.title: Potential OCR error in Proposition 4's IO complexity formula
comments.explanation: The OCR output shows the sparsity factor _s_ appended after the exponent notation without a clear multiplication symbol. While the intended meaning (multiplying by _s_) is clear from the context and the prose that follows ('yields a direct improvement by the sparsity'), the formatting could be confusing. The correct mathematical typesetting would likely be Θ( N d + N^2 d^2 M^{-1} s ) or similar. This is a minor typesetting artifact, not a substantive error.

comments.title: Potential inconsistency in block size subscript notation for sparsity mask
comments.explanation: The authors denote the sparsity mask as **M̃** ∈ {0,1}^{N×N} with the tilde, but then later they refer to a reduced mask **M** ∈ {0,1}^{N/B_r × N/B_c} without the tilde. In the passage, they write 'for some **M** ∈{0,1}^{N/B_r × N/B_c}'. This means the notation **M** is overloaded: earlier in the paper, **M** is used to denote the size of SRAM, and here it represents a block-sparsity mask. Within this specific passage, the context makes it clear that **M** here refers to the mask, not the SRAM size, but this could be momentarily confusing for a reader tracking multiple uses of the same symbol. The SRAM size is typically denoted by _M_ (italic), while the mask is **M** (bold), which helps distinguish them, but the OCR may obscure this distinction.

comments.title: Potential oversight in block-sparse softmax definition with masking
comments.explanation: The definition of the masked attention uses -∞ for zero entries in the mask, which effectively sets e^{-∞} = 0 in the softmax computation. However, this is only correct if the softmax is computed numerically with appropriate handling of -∞ (e.g., using a large negative constant or separate masking logic). In practice, if the softmax is computed naively with -∞, numerical issues can arise (e.g., NaN from 0/0 if the entire row is masked). The authors may handle this correctly in their CUDA implementation, but the prose description glosses over this detail. Additionally, for block-sparse attention, the masking is typically applied at the block level, meaning all entries in a block are either all present or all set to -∞. The definition given applies elementwise, which is technically correct but doesn't emphasize the block structure. This could lead a reader to implement an inefficient elementwise mask instead of an efficient block-level mask. progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive Original (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and impactful contribution, addressing the memory and speed bottlenecks of Transformer attention through an elegant IO-aware tiling strategy. The quality is high, with rigorous IO complexity analysis, careful implementation, and strong empirical results demonstrating substantial wall-clock speedups and reduced memory usage. The clarity is generally excellent: the motivation, algorithmic innovation (tiling with online softmax and recomputation), and the distinction from FLOP-oriented approaches are well articulated. However, several issues merit deeper scrutiny. First, the claim of "exact attention" deserves nuance: the numerical stability of the recomputation-based backward pass and the blockwise softmax (which relies on rescaling) should be explicitly addressed, as finite-precision arithmetic could introduce minor deviations from exact attention, especially for long sequences. Second, the IO complexity lower bound relies on assumptions about SRAM size relative to problem dimensions (e.g., \(M = \Omega(d)\)); it is not clear that the bound holds for all practical regimes, and the optimality claim might be overstated. Third, the paper’s focus on NVIDIA GPU HBM/SRAM hierarchy limits generalizability—the approach may not transfer directly to other accelerators (e.g., TPUs) with different memory hierarchies or programming models. Fourth, while the block-sparse extension is a valuable addition, the title’s emphasis on "exact attention" is somewhat misleading since the sparse variant is approximate; a clearer distinction in the abstract or introduction would help. Finally, the end-to-end speedup benchmarks, while impressive, are against specific baselines (e.g., MLPerf 1.1 record, standard PyTorch attention); comparisons with other optimized implementations (e.g., xformers, FasterTransformer) would strengthen the evaluation. Despite these concerns, the paper is a landmark contribution that reorients attention optimization toward memory-access efficiency, and the identified issues are mostly avenues for future refinement rather than fundamental flaws.

comments.title: Incorrect use of Ω for standard attention complexity
comments.quote: as compared to Ω( _𝑁𝑑_ + _𝑁_[2] ) of standard attention.
comments.explanation: The authors state the HBM access complexity of standard attention as Ω(𝑁𝑑 + 𝑁²), using big-Omega which denotes a lower bound. However, the intended meaning is to give an upper bound (or exact bound) for comparison with FlashAttention's upper bound O(𝑁²𝑑²/𝑀). Later in the paper (Theorem 2) the standard attention complexity is correctly given as Θ(𝑁𝑑 + 𝑁²). Using Ω here is technically a lower bound, not an upper bound, which could confuse readers about the direction of the comparison. The context resolves the intended comparison, but the notation is mildly inconsistent with standard usage in complexity analysis.

> 1FLASHATTENTION code is available at `https://github.com/HazyResearch/flash-attention`

2

We empirically validate that

The stray '2' on its own line likely corresponds to a superscript or footnote number that should attach to the preceding code URL or the following sentence. While not a mathematical error, this OCR artifact could confuse a reader about whether '2' is part of the bullet list or a footnote reference. The context makes it clear that the bullet list starting with 'Faster Model Training.' follows, so the '2' is almost certainly a misplaced footnote marker. This is a formatting artifact, not a content error, and does not affect the scientific claims.

comments.title: Incorrect formula for softmax decomposition
comments.explanation: The softmax formula is written as `softmax( x ) := _ℓ[𝑓]_ ([(] _𝑥[𝑥]_ )[)] _[.]_`. The intended meaning is softmax(x) = f(x) / ℓ(x), as standard. The OCR has mangled the expression: the denominator appears to be ℓ(f(x)) rather than ℓ(x). However, from the context and the later consistent usage (e.g., 'softmax( x ) = _[𝑓]_[(] _[𝑥]_[)] _ℓ_ ( _𝑥_ ) _[.]_' for the concatenated case), it is clear the author intends the standard ratio. The issue is purely OCR corruption of the notation; the correct mathematical intent is unambiguous from the surrounding text. No real problem for a careful reader.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of ℓ(x) in the softmax decomposition
comments.explanation: The equation for ℓ(x) when concatenating two vectors introduces an exponentiation in the update, but the initial definition of ℓ(x) was 'ℓ( x ) := ∑︁ _𝑖 f( x )_ i', i.e., the sum of the exponentials (the numerator before normalization). The notation here switches: it writes ℓ(x) for the combined result as a function of ℓ of the parts, which is consistent with the online softmax trick. However, the exponentiation factors in the update are correct (they rescale by the difference in max). The only potential confusion is that the same symbol ℓ is used for both the raw sum-of-exponentials and the updated version; this is standard in the literature. No error.

comments.quote: On˜ chip, compute _𝑚𝑖𝑗_ = rowmax( **S** _𝑖𝑗_ ) ∈ R _[𝐵][𝑟]_ , **P**[˜] _𝑖𝑗_ = exp( **S** _𝑖𝑗_ − _𝑚𝑖𝑗_ ) ∈ R _[𝐵][𝑟]_[×] _[𝐵][𝑐]_ (pointwise), _ℓ𝑖𝑗_ = rowsum( ˜ **P** _𝑖𝑗_ ) ∈ R _[𝐵] 𝑟_ .

comments.title: Claim that no exact attention algorithm can improve on HBM accesses over all SRAM sizes may be overstated
comments.quote: Proposition 3. ... There does not exist an algorithm to compute exact attention with 𝑜(𝑁^2 𝑑^2 𝑀^{−1}) HBM accesses for all 𝑀 in the range [𝑑,𝑁𝑑].
comments.explanation: The proposition claims a lower bound over 'all M in the range [d, Nd]'. The proof sketch only considers the case M = Θ(Nd), showing that Ω(Nd) accesses are needed. However, the lower bound form 'o(N^2 d^2 / M)' for that specific M yields Ω(N^2 d^2 / (Nd)) = Ω(Nd), which matches the trivial lower bound. The proposition states 'for all M in the range', but the proof only addresses one endpoint. A true parameterized lower bound would need to show that for any M, the complexity cannot be o(N^2 d^2 / M). The authors acknowledge this limitation ('we leave proving parameterized complexity lower bounds...as exciting future work'), so the proposition as stated is technically correct (it only rules out algorithms that are o(N^2 d^2 / M) *for all M simultaneously*), but the phrasing could mislead a reader into thinking they have proven a tight lower bound for each M individually. This is a minor issue of precision.

comments.title: Potential oversight in block-sparse softmax definition with masking

In practice, if the softmax is computed naively with -∞, numerical issues can arise (e.g., NaN from 0/0 if the entire row is masked). The authors may handle this correctly in their CUDA implementation, but the prose description glosses over this detail. Additionally, for block-sparse attention, the masking is typically applied at the block level, meaning all entries in a block are either all present or all set to -∞. The definition given applies elementwise, which is technically correct but doesn't emphasize the block structure. This could lead a reader to implement an inefficient elementwise mask instead of an efficient block-level mask. However, since the context emphasizes block structure and the algorithm skips zero blocks entirely (not computing them at all), the practicality is clear.
